text:
This study constitutes the first attempt to provide a comprehensive inventory and analysis of honour programmes and schemes of excellence in higher education across the eleven countries of the Benelux, Nordic and German-speaking regions of Northern Europe. Honour programs in the US have a long and well established tradition. Other countries, however, have only recently started to develop their own programmes of study for gifted and highly motivated students. Based on an exploratory multi-country study involving more than 300 higher education institutions this study found existence of honour programmes at 72 institutions.

The report concludes that the Netherlands is the European frontrunner for honours education, while the other countries are engaged in a different way due to different ideological and structural factors. Among other things, the conflict between the egalitarian social values that underpin the Scandinavian countries – the "Law of Jante" – and the need to be able to compete internationally in a knowledge economy, are significant factors in the countries' programmes for talent development and thus also for the terminology, the level and organization of the support provided by government and other actors.

After looking at different national systems of education, recruitment strategies and financing, the study reveals how each honours programme acts as a 'laboratory for innovation' for a significant part of the curriculum offered in mainstream universities. The study underlines the necessity of developing a shared language for honour’s colleges worldwide as well as of more intense and transnational networking. Finally, the study concludes that, while many claim that developing the best students of each country amounts to excluding egalitarian democracy, promoting excellence among students should by no means be seen as an antagonistic aim to a fairer society, because excellent students may turn into excellent citizens and necessary future world-leaders and agents of creativity.
